fund.name: HDFC Flexi Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: We'll start with a solid large and mid-cap fund that can navigate India's economic cycles smoothly. HDFC has one of the longest track records, a huge corpus over 32 years, and a proven team. It's like the anchor of the plan — keeping your rides comfortable while you get back into India, with returns of around 20% per year historically.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Large and Midcap Fund - Direct Plan Growth

allocations.rationale: After 10 years, you'll be excited to see India's growth story again. This Motilal Oswal fund has a 6-year record of grabbing speed, with 27% returns over three years. It focuses more on mid-cap stocks that could be the next big winners.

fund.name: Nippon India Growth Mid Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: Mid-cap funds have higher returns but more risk. After the tough times of the pandemic, Nippon's experienced team has shown you can stay in a high-growth area. In fact, this fund has delivered 22% per year over five years.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Midcap Fund-Direct Plan-Growth Option

allocations.rationale: For longer-term growth, mid-cap stocks can give you big upside, but they fall harder during a crash. Motilal Oswal has been around since 2011 and focuses on consistent growth. Their record so far is strong — 23.8% per year over five years.

fund.name: Nippon India Multi Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: Multi-cap funds allow the fund manager to hop between small, mid, and large-cap stocks as the economy changes. Nippon has thrown together a balanced mix that fits your moderate risk. It has been a top performer, averaging 21.3% per year over five years.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: ICICI has been around since the 80s and has survived many ups and downs. Their Large & Mid Cap fund balances aggressive growth with stability. Over 27 years, this fund has averaged over 20% per year, making it a good catch-up vehicle for someone rejoining the market.

fund.name: Sundaram Long Term Tax Advantage Fund Series I Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: Since you're in the 30% tax slab and want to use your 80C limit, this ELSS fund is a win-win. It gives you a tax deduction of up to 1.5 lakh per year (saving up to ₹46,800 in tax for you), and it's also invested in good mid and large-cap stocks. It also has a 3-year lock-in, so you need to commit for the short term. Its 5-year returns are incredible — 25.7% per year!

fund.name: Aditya Birla Sun Life Medium Term Plan - Growth - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: As you look to your goal of retiring at 62, you'll need safer instruments. This medium-duration debt fund is more stable than equities and gives you returns around 10% per year, which helps pad your emergency fund or contribute to your retirement corpus without taking big swings.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on a platform like Groww, Kuvera, or Coin (apps familiar to US expats and Indians alike). Step 2: Set up automatic SIPs in these eight funds as listed above for ₹40,000 monthly. Step 3: Start with a small corpus (e.g., ₹4.5 million in India) by transferring it to the same account and investing in these funds as a 'lumpsum'. Step 4: Review the plan every quarter to ensure the portfolio is still aligned with your goals.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Check if all SIPs are running and if you're seeing expected growth. Review your emergency fund setup.
After 1 year: Compare the performance of each fund with its category average. Exit any fund that has underperformed for 2 consecutive quarters.
After 3 years: Rebalance to target return allocation (50% equities, 50% debt) or exit if your goals change.
Yearly: Check if any of your SIPs have exceeded your target return or dragged your returns down.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Every year, check if any category has grown more than expected (e.g., mid-caps taking off). If a category like mid-caps has grown too large, move some money back to safer debt options or reduce the SIP in that fund. Always aim for a balanced portfolio — too much in one category creates 'risk concentration'.
plan.rationale: At 45 with 17 years left, you're in a great spot: long enough to recover from market dips but short enough to need safety now. Your return target of 5Cr requires a compound return of around 8% per year. With India growing at 7-8% annually, we can aim for 12-15% returns over the long term. Since you're a 30% tax bracket investor, ELSS and equity funds are tax-efficient, and intermediate debt funds help cushion volatility.
risks: Equity markets can drop 20-30% in a bad year (like 2008 or 2020), but history shows they recover over 5-7 years. Avoid panic selling during crashes.
Debt funds are not risk-free — interest rate changes or credit downgrades can hurt returns.
Inflation erosion: Even at 7% returns, inflation can eat away 2-3% of your purchasing power. You need returns of 8%+ to stay ahead of inflation.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% each year to match salary growth and keep up with inflation.

aps.reasoning:
The plan selects 100% active equity funds (Flexi, Mid, Multi-cap, Large&Mid-cap) with active manager mandates, resulting in 0% passive equity. Your portfolio is highly concentrated in active money.

1. Passive Instrument (0.0): All equity allocations are to active mutual funds (Flexi, Mid, Multi-cap). No index funds are present.

2. Turnover (0.4): While the long-term intent is buy-and-hold, the reviews explicitly mandate checking for underperformance every quarter and exiting 'challengers,' creating a moderate-to-high assessment around 0.4.

3. Cost Emphasis (0.1): The plan ignores expense ratios entirely, focusing only on historical returns and manager tenure.

4. Research vs Cost (0.05): The plan justifies active funds using historical alpha and manager track records but makes no mention of any individual stock/company research. It is purely fund-based.

5. Time Horizon (0.7): The plan targets 17 years of compounding with SIPs, aligning well with long-term goals despite the aggressive annual review cycles.

6. Portfolio Activeness (0.0): All equity funds have implicit tracking errors well above 3% and betas >1.0 (active mandates), confirming the portfolio is 100% genuinely active.
